author: @VeniceInventors
text: I've been toying with making time lapses of 3D prints, but didn't like how the model and head keep moving in each frame. I watched how some people add g-code to move the head out of the way at the end of each layer, pushing a switch at the far end to trigger the camera, while others use special cameras feeding the g-code to the printer, allowing it to control when to take the shot. In both case the result is amazing as the head and model remain static and the model just grows as if coming out of a replicator in Star Trek. Then it occurred to me that a simpler trick would be to trigger the camera at the end of each motion of the Z axis motor. It would require adding a sensor on the motor wire or shaft and pair it with a micro-controller to trigger the camera or capture, but it might be worth the extra effort to get nice shots without modifying the g-code or using an expensive camera.

author: @VeniceInventors
text: I've been toying with making time lapses of 3D prints, but didn't like how the model and head keep moving in each frame. In both case the result is amazing as the head and model remain static and the model just grows as if coming out of a replicator in Star Trek. Then it occurred to me that a simpler trick would be to trigger the camera at the end of each motion of the Z axis motor. author: @010Larry010
text: Very interestring. I joined the 3D printing world about 18 months ago after purchasing an Artillery Hornet. I had an old HP Chromebox which was happy to run Lubuntu Linux along with Cura. Despite having no previous Linux experience, the installation went well with no particular difficulties. The included Firefox browser running Tinkercad completes the setup. Bootup is very quick and it's proved to be very stable. Cura has been updated a couple of times to keep pace with the latest version.

author: @gordslater
text: Just be aware that some distro package repos for Cura will be rather out of date, for example, downloading the appimage (and making it executable to make it run) direct from the Cura website gives me v5.6.0, but using MX-Linux repo (installing it via  apt) only gives me version 4.13.0

author: @WilliamFrick
text: I have been using Linux for 15 years and 3 D printing for 10+.  You will find that all slicers and print interfaces have a linux version.  Klipper itself is a linux program.   It usually comes down to what flavour you like.  For me it is Ubuntu as all things needed to be productive come with the base install.
